demographics.living_situation: lives at home; adult child provides support and is present tonight

opening_complaint.verbatim_patient_text: Caregiver (adult child) calling: "They completely forgot who I was for ten minutes, left the gas on, and couldn't form a sentence. I thought they were having a stroke. They're on chemo and I'm terrified something is really wrong."
opening_complaint.stated_reason_for_presenting_today: A sudden frightening episode tonight where my parent was confused, wandered the hallway and couldn't speak properly, and left the stove running — it has settled after about half an hour but I don't know if it's safe to wait until morning.

symptom_narrative.onset: Sudden peak tonight, on a background of about 2 months of gradual decline
symptom_narrative.duration: The acute episode lasted around 30 minutes and has now settled; the background fogginess has been building for ~2 months
symptom_narrative.character: Confused and disoriented — didn't recognise me for about ten minutes, wandered the hallway, and couldn't put a sentence together (halting, stuck for words). Very distressed afterwards.
symptom_narrative.severity_patient_description: The worst episode so far — it really frightened both of us
symptom_narrative.location_as_patient_describes: It's their thinking and speech — not any one part of the body
symptom_narrative.timing_pattern: Comes and goes; seems worse when they're tired or stressed, then a big spike tonight
symptom_narrative.what_makes_it_better: It eased off on its own after about half an hour with rest and reassurance
symptom_narrative.what_makes_it_worse: Tiredness and stress seem to make the fogginess worse
symptom_narrative.associated_symptoms_volunteered: No fever that we've noticed, no fall or bump to the head, and I didn't see any face droop or one-sided arm weakness. Their speech is back to normal now, just a bit slow to answer.
symptom_narrative.previous_episodes: Milder 'brain fog' and trouble juggling tasks over the past couple of months since the chemo, but nothing this severe before
symptom_narrative.functional_impact: Usually manages their own basic daily tasks, but tonight couldn't manage cooking safely and left the gas on — I'm worried about accidents at home

past_medical_history.condition_as_patient_states: Cancer of the lymph glands (lymphoma), currently being treated
past_medical_history.how_managed: On a course of chemotherapy — this is their fourth round

current_medications_as_reported.name_as_patient_states: Chemotherapy for the cancer (I don't know the exact drug names)

social_history_volunteered.relevant_life_context: Adult child is the main carer, present on-site tonight, exhausted and very worried; concerned about leaving their parent alone with the stove and medications

objective_data_offered.value: 36.8 degrees on our home thermometer, so no fever that we can measure
objective_data_offered.reliability_caveat: single reading on a home thermometer; a normal home temperature does not exclude infection in someone on chemotherapy

objective_data_offered.value: pulse feels around 88 and regular when I check their wrist
objective_data_offered.reliability_caveat: manually counted by an untrained carer

objective_data_offered.value: I didn't see any face droop or one-sided arm weakness, and their speech is back to normal now — just slower to answer than usual
objective_data_offered.reliability_caveat: a carer's impression is NOT a neurological examination and cannot rule out a stroke or other neurological cause

objective_data_offered.value: patient is awake and tearful, speaking in full sentences now but seems slow to respond and to follow multi-step requests
the gas stove has now been turned off by the carer; the patient had earlier been found wandering the hallway
